questions.scenario: A release pipeline submits a Deployment requesting three `notification-api` replicas. Seconds later, the API record shows `spec.replicas: 3` and `status.availableReplicas: 1`. Which interpretation is most accurate while the rollout is still progressing?

options.text: The team requested one replica, and Kubernetes plans to increase that request to three.
The scheduler has already placed three ready Pods, but the Deployment spec has not caught up.
The API server rejected the object because `spec` and `status` contain different numbers.
The desired state is three replicas, while Kubernetes currently reports only one available replica.

questions.explanation: The team writes desired state in `spec`, while Kubernetes reports observed state in `status`. A temporary difference is expected during reconciliation. The scheduler does not rewrite the desired replica count, and different spec and status values do not by themselves mean rejection.

questions.scenario: Four new worker Pods remain `Pending`. Their events say no node has enough available memory for each Pod's request. The application image starts successfully in a smaller development cluster. What should the team investigate first?

options.text: Node capacity and the worker memory requests, because the scheduler needs a node where each Pod fits.
The Service DNS name, because DNS resolution determines whether the scheduler can reserve memory.
The readiness endpoint, because failed readiness prevents a Pod from being assigned to a node.
The Deployment image history, because an old image revision consumes the requested memory before scheduling.

questions.explanation: The scheduler uses resource requests when deciding whether a Pod fits on a node, so node capacity and request sizing are the direct evidence. Service DNS and readiness matter after placement. Image history does not reserve memory for an unscheduled Pod.

questions.scenario: A Deployment creates three ready Pods labeled `app: notification-api` and `component: api`. The internal Service resolves correctly but selects `app: notification-api` and `component: backend`, so no application endpoints appear. What is the most direct repair?

options.text: Increase Deployment replicas so the Service can select a Pod after the fourth copy starts.
Align the Service selector with the approved Pod labels so both use `component: api`.
Replace the ClusterIP Service with direct Pod IPs recorded in the caller configuration.
Raise the API memory limit so Kubernetes can translate `backend` into the Pod's `api` label.

questions.explanation: Selectors match exact label key-value pairs, so the Service and Pod identity must align. More replicas do not repair a mismatch. Direct Pod IPs discard the stable Service contract, and resource limits do not transform labels.

questions.scenario: Version `1.8.0` creates new API Pods, but each fails `/ready` while the old Pods remain ready. Callers still reach the old version through the same Service name. Which explanation best describes the cooperating objects?

options.text: The Service owns both ReplicaSets and changes the Deployment image only after every request succeeds.
The nodes keep the old image because a Deployment cannot create old and new Pods at the same time.
The Deployment creates replacement Pods, readiness keeps failed new Pods out of traffic, and the Service continues routing to ready matches.
The API server copies user requests into the old Pods until the new image changes its labels automatically.

questions.explanation: The Deployment controls replacement, the readiness result determines traffic eligibility, and the Service keeps a stable route to ready selected Pods. Services do not own ReplicaSets, Deployments can run old and new revisions during a rollout, and the API server does not proxy application requests.

questions.scenario: Users report slow notification requests after a rollout. The Deployment has three desired and three available replicas. Two slow Pods are both on `worker-03`, while the third Pod on `worker-01` is healthy. The Service selector matches all three Pods, and every Pod reports ready. Which two investigation moves follow the article's object relationships?

options.text: Inspect `worker-03` for pressure, network, disk, or runtime trouble because the symptom follows one node.
Rewrite the Service name because a stable name cannot route to Pods on more than one node.
Confirm resource usage and limits for the affected Pods because CPU throttling or memory pressure can create runtime symptoms.
Change the Deployment selector for the current image so each rollout receives a new workload identity.

questions.explanation: The node correlation makes `worker-03` a strong investigation target, and resource evidence can explain throttling or pressure on those Pods. A Service can route across nodes, and changing the stable Deployment selector per image would break the ownership contract rather than diagnose the slowdown.
